name: Second Harvest Food Bank
mission: Summer Meals Second Harvest works with school districts and community partners to maximize the use of federally funded school meals.
theoryOfChange: Distributing nutritious food while advocating for systemic policy change and providing nutrition education creates a foundation for a healthy, productive life and ends hunger.

scoreSummary: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley shows strong impact and good alignment (minor risk deductions applied).

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Good donor alignment with manageable risk. Evidence quality is solid but still evolving in places.

keyConcerns.headline: 74% of reported revenue is noncash (gifts-in-kind)
keyConcerns.detail: Noncash contributions inflate reported program ratios. Cash-adjusted metrics better reflect actual spending.

zakat.claim_evidence: No explicit mention of "zakat" on the donation pages or any other part of the provided search results for Second Harvest Food Bank organizations. The donation options listed include one-time, monthly, stocks, fundraisers, legacy giving, and gift-in-kind, but do not specify zakat as a donation type. (Source: https://www.secondharvest.ca/get-involved/make-a-donation)

all_citations.claim: The charity maintains a perfect Charity Navigator score of 100.0/100.
The organization reported total revenue of $286,205,684.
The organization focuses on maximizing federally funded school meals and food rescue.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The organization earns a 67/100 due to its perfect financial health and transparency scores, though its overall impact score is moderated by the high cost of operating in an expensive geographic region.
areas_for_improvement: High cost per beneficiary relative to global hunger relief benchmarks
Limited specific alignment with traditional Muslim donor preferences

dimension_explanations.alignment: The organization addresses the urgent issue of extreme poverty and food insecurity within its service area [3].
dimension_explanations.impact: The charity demonstrates high effectiveness with a perfect Charity Navigator score of 100.0/100, reflecting strong accountability and financial health [1]. While it maintains a high program expense ratio of 94.8%, the cost per beneficiary is approximately $528.78, which is reflective of the high-cost environment of Silicon Valley [2][3].

baseline_narrative.headline: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley provides critical food security through a highly efficient distribution network and federally funded meal programs.
strengths: Exceptional financial efficiency with a program expense ratio of 94.8%
Strong liquidity with 9.5 months of working capital
Highly efficient fundraising operations costing only $0.03 per $1 raised
baseline_narrative.summary: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley operates as a primary food bank for Santa Clara and San Mateo counties, managing a total revenue of $286,205,684 to combat local hunger [2]. The organization focuses on maximizing federally funded school meals and maintaining a robust network of community partners to distribute groceries to those in need [3].

areas_for_improvement.detail: While efficient for a US-based food bank, the <cite id="17">$528.78 cost per beneficiary</cite> is high compared to international hunger relief benchmarks.
Approximately <cite id="18">74% of revenue is derived from non-cash contributions</cite> (GIK), which can create valuation volatility.

bbb_assessment.summary: Meets all 20 BBB Wise Giving Alliance Standards for Charity Accountability

case_against.mitigation_notes: The organization maintains nearly 9.5 months of working capital to mitigate revenue fluctuations.
case_against.summary: The primary limitations include a <cite id="24">-5.8% revenue CAGR</cite> over three years and a high reliance on <cite id="18">in-kind food donations</cite>.

confidence.notes: Extremely high data availability and third-party verification.

alignment.improvement: Alignment could be strengthened by <cite id="23">expanding services to underserved geographies</cite> with limited existing nonprofit coverage.

credibility.explanation: Donors can place high trust in SHSV due to its <cite id="15">100.0/100 from Charity Navigator score</cite>, <cite id="19">20/20 BBB Standards compliance</cite>, and <cite id="16">Platinum Candid Seal</cite>.
credibility.improvement: To further enhance credibility, the organization could <cite id="20">document a clear theory of change</cite> with an explicit causal pathway for long-term food security.

impact.explanation: Impact is driven by a <cite id="6">94.6% program ratio</cite> and the successful distribution of <cite id="11">94 million meals</cite> annually.
impact.improvement: The charity could improve its impact score by <cite id="20">formalizing its theory of change</cite> to better measure long-term outcomes beyond meal counts.

rich_narrative.headline: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley provides critical food security through a highly efficient distribution network and federally funded meal programs.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who prioritize local community impact in Northern California and value extreme financial efficiency in food distribution.
donor_motivations: Want to support local hunger relief in Silicon Valley
Value organizations with a perfect 100/100 rating from third-party evaluators
Prefer charities with very low fundraising overhead
Interested in supporting large-scale food rescue operations
giving_considerations: Donations are sadaqah-eligible but not verified for zakat
The high cost per beneficiary reflects the expensive local operating environment
Large-scale institutional model rather than a small community grassroots approach
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking international impact or those specifically looking to fulfill zakat obligations through a verified religious pathway.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade B reflects <cite id="25">4 tracked outcome metrics</cite> and <cite id="26">2 external evaluation documents</cite>.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: SHSV uses a network of partners and federal programs to provide immediate food access while advocating for long-term policy changes.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that the millions of pounds of food rescued actually reach those in nutritional need.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: While revenue has slightly declined, the high cost of living in the region ensures a constant need for expanded services.

organizational_capacity.geographic_reach: San Mateo and Santa Clara Counties

strengths.detail: The organization directs <cite id="6">94.6% of its total expenses</cite> toward program services, ensuring that the vast majority of funds directly support food distribution.
SHSV maintains a highly lean development operation, spending only <cite id="8">$0.03 per $1 raised</cite>, which is among the lowest in the humanitarian sector.
The charity holds <cite id="10">9.5 months of working capital</cite>, providing a significant buffer to maintain services during economic downturns.

strengths_deep_dive: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley's operational scale is evidenced by its ability to provide <cite id="11">94 million meals</cite> and rescue <cite id="12">84 million pounds of food</cite> in a single year. Their governance is equally robust, featuring a <cite id="13">17-member board</cite> with <cite id="14">94% independent membership</cite>, ensuring high levels of oversight. This combination of scale and transparency has earned them a <cite id="15">perfect 100/100 overall score</cite> from Charity Navigator and a <cite id="16">Platinum Seal of Transparency</cite> from Candid.
rich_narrative.summary: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley (SHSV) serves as a critical food security backbone for <cite id="1">San Mateo and Santa Clara counties</cite>, operating with a mission to ensure that anyone who needs a healthy meal can get one. Founded in <cite id="2">1974</cite>, the organization has evolved into one of the largest food banks in the nation, managing <cite id="3">$286,205,684 in total revenue</cite> to combat local hunger. Their operational model is highly decentralized, utilizing a <cite id="4">robust network of community partners</cite> and school districts to distribute groceries and maximize the reach of <cite id="5">federally funded school meal programs</cite>. The charity demonstrates exceptional fiscal discipline, maintaining a <cite id="6">program expense ratio of 94.8%</cite>, which significantly exceeds the <cite id="7">industry standard of 75%</cite>. This efficiency is further highlighted by their fundraising prowess, as it costs the organization only <cite id="8">$0.03 to raise every $1</cite> in public support. Beyond immediate relief, SHSV engages in <cite id="9">CalFresh outreach</cite> and advocacy to address the root causes of food insecurity in one of the most expensive regions in the United States.
